patient_profile: 72M, BMI 24, non-smoker, controlled T2DM (HbA1c 6.2%), daily exercise, no cardiovascular history, LDL 90 mg/dL on statin.
red_flag: Stage IV pancreatic cancer diagnosed 2 months ago
moderation_ground_truth: Red flag is severe but profile shows otherwise excellent baseline health; model should not ignore controlled metabolic status.
model_reasoning: Patient has stage IV pancreatic cancer which carries a median survival of 3-6 months. However, his excellent baseline health — controlled diabetes with HbA1c 6.2%, normal BMI, active lifestyle, and good lipid profile — may support better tolerance of treatment and modestly extend expected survival compared to average. The cancer dominates the prognosis but the strong baseline should moderate the magnitude of the negative adjustment.

human_label.summary_sentence: Integrates cancer severity with baseline health metrics to produce a proportionate negative delta.

patient_profile: 65F, BMI 22, never-smoker, breast cancer Stage I diagnosed 6 months ago, post-lumpectomy, on tamoxifen, ER+/PR+, no lymph node involvement, walks 5 km daily.
moderation_ground_truth: Stage I ER+ breast cancer post-surgery with tamoxifen has >90% 5-year survival; model should not over-penalize.
model_reasoning: The patient has breast cancer. Cancer significantly reduces life expectancy. Breast cancer is a serious disease with high mortality. I estimate a substantial reduction in lifespan.

human_label.summary_sentence: Pure keyword reflex to 'cancer' — ignores Stage I, ER+ status, successful surgery, and tamoxifen.

patient_profile: 58M, BMI 31, ex-smoker (quit 15 years ago), mild COPD (FEV1 72%), controlled hypertension on lisinopril, moderate alcohol (7 drinks/week), sedentary.
red_flag: Family history of lung cancer (father, age 70)
moderation_ground_truth: Family history is a risk factor but father diagnosed at 70 suggests moderate genetic risk; patient quit smoking 15 years ago which substantially reduces risk.
model_reasoning: Father had lung cancer, and the patient has COPD from prior smoking. While the patient quit 15 years ago — which significantly lowers risk — the combination of family history and residual COPD warrants monitoring. However, the family history alone, especially with diagnosis at age 70, does not justify a large prediction shift. The sedentary lifestyle and mild obesity are arguably more impactful on overall longevity than the family history.

human_label.summary_sentence: Correctly weighs family history against smoking cessation and prioritizes lifestyle factors over genetic risk.

patient_profile: 80F, BMI 20, non-smoker, no chronic conditions, independent ADLs, lives alone, walks daily, cognitively intact (MMSE 28/30).
moderation_ground_truth: Age alone is not a red flag when functional status is excellent; model should recognize exceptional health for age.
model_reasoning: Patient is 80 years old. At this age, life expectancy is significantly reduced. The average 80-year-old woman has about 9 years remaining. I'll apply a standard age-based reduction. Blood pressure is probably high and she likely has undiagnosed conditions.

human_label.summary_sentence: Ignores excellent functional status, fabricates 'probably high' blood pressure and 'undiagnosed conditions.'

patient_profile: 45M, BMI 27, non-smoker, no medications, mildly elevated LDL (145 mg/dL), BP 128/82, family history of longevity (both parents alive at 85+), software engineer, moderate exercise.
moderation_ground_truth: LDL 145 is borderline; with no other risk factors and family longevity, this is a minimal concern.
model_reasoning: The patient has elevated cholesterol which is a risk factor for cardiovascular disease. However, with LDL at 145 — only mildly above optimal — no smoking, borderline-normal blood pressure, and a strong family history of longevity, the cardiovascular risk is low. Lifestyle modification alone could address this. The family history of longevity is a significant protective factor that outweighs this mild lipid finding.

human_label.summary_sentence: Appropriately contextualizes mild LDL elevation against family longevity and absence of compounding risks.

patient_profile: 55M, BMI 34, current smoker (1 pack/day x 30 years), T2DM (HbA1c 8.9%), uncontrolled hypertension (BP 165/100), sedentary, LDL 188 mg/dL, eGFR 52.
red_flag: Recent diagnosis of peripheral artery disease
moderation_ground_truth: PAD on top of uncontrolled multi-system disease is appropriately severe; large negative delta is justified.
model_reasoning: The patient has multiple severely uncontrolled risk factors: heavy smoking, poorly controlled diabetes, uncontrolled hypertension, dyslipidemia, and now PAD indicating established vascular disease. The eGFR of 52 suggests Stage 3 CKD. PAD in this context is a marker of systemic atherosclerosis, not an isolated finding. Each risk factor compounds the others. A substantial negative adjustment is warranted.

human_label.summary_sentence: Correctly synthesizes PAD as a systemic atherosclerosis marker compounding multiple uncontrolled risk factors.

patient_profile: 40F, BMI 23, non-smoker, no chronic conditions, marathon runner, vegetarian, BP 110/70, total cholesterol 180, family history unremarkable.
moderation_ground_truth: Tension headaches in an otherwise perfectly healthy person are benign; no prediction impact warranted.
model_reasoning: The patient reports headaches. Headaches can be a symptom of brain tumors, aneurysms, or hypertension. This warrants further investigation. The headaches could indicate an underlying serious neurological condition that may reduce life expectancy. I recommend an MRI to rule out intracranial pathology.

human_label.summary_sentence: Catastrophizes benign tension headaches into brain tumors, ignoring perfect health profile and normal BP.

patient_profile: 68M, BMI 26, ex-smoker (quit 5 years ago), COPD (FEV1 58%), on tiotropium and fluticasone, moderate exercise tolerance, occasional exacerbations (1-2/year).
red_flag: COPD exacerbation requiring hospitalization last year
moderation_ground_truth: One hospitalization in a year is moderate; model should note treatment compliance and exercise tolerance as mitigating factors.
model_reasoning: The patient had a COPD exacerbation requiring hospitalization. His FEV1 of 58% indicates moderate-to-severe obstruction. However, he quit smoking 5 years ago, is on appropriate dual therapy, and maintains moderate exercise tolerance. The exacerbation frequency of 1-2/year is concerning but not unusual for this stage. The hospitalization signals disease progression but doesn't dramatically alter the trajectory given his current management.

human_label.summary_sentence: Contextualizes well but understates exacerbation severity and applies a somewhat high delta for managed COPD.

patient_profile: 50F, BMI 29, non-smoker, hypothyroidism on levothyroxine (TSH normal), mild anxiety on sertraline, prediabetic (HbA1c 5.9%), walks 30 min/day, BP 122/78.
moderation_ground_truth: HbA1c 5.9% with active lifestyle is early-stage and highly reversible; model should not treat this as established diabetes.
model_reasoning: The patient has prediabetes. Diabetes is a major risk factor for cardiovascular disease, kidney failure, blindness, and amputation. This patient is likely to progress to full diabetes. The combination with her BMI of 29 suggests metabolic syndrome. I expect significant reduction in lifespan due to diabetic complications.

human_label.summary_sentence: Conflates prediabetes with established diabetes, fabricates progression certainty, and ignores active lifestyle.

patient_profile: 62F, BMI 25, non-smoker, well-controlled rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate (DAS28 2.4), no organ damage, regular swimming, BP 118/72, no cardiovascular history.
moderation_ground_truth: RA in remission (DAS28 <2.6) on standard DMARD has minimal excess mortality; model should not over-penalize.
model_reasoning: Rheumatoid arthritis increases cardiovascular risk and can cause systemic inflammation. The patient is on methotrexate which has hepatotoxicity risks. However, her disease is well-controlled with a DAS28 of 2.4 indicating remission. She exercises regularly, has normal BP, and no organ damage. The methotrexate is actually cardioprotective in RA through inflammation reduction. The well-controlled RA adds only a small incremental risk.

human_label.summary_sentence: Recognizes RA remission and methotrexate's cardioprotective role, producing a minimal and proportionate delta.

patient_profile: 70M, BMI 28, non-smoker, atrial fibrillation on apixaban (CHA2DS2-VASc 3), controlled hypertension on amlodipine, mild aortic stenosis, walks 2 km daily.
moderation_ground_truth: AF on appropriate anticoagulation with controlled comorbidities has manageable risk; mild AS needs monitoring but is not immediately life-limiting.
model_reasoning: The patient has atrial fibrillation which increases stroke risk. He also has aortic stenosis which can lead to sudden death. The combination is dangerous. He's on apixaban which helps with stroke prevention, and his AF appears rate-controlled. The mild aortic stenosis requires monitoring but isn't currently critical. His daily walking suggests reasonable functional capacity. The AF adds moderate risk but is being appropriately managed.

human_label.summary_sentence: Correctly notes anticoagulation but overstates aortic stenosis danger with 'sudden death' language for mild AS.

patient_profile: 48M, BMI 22, non-smoker, HIV+ on ART (viral load undetectable x 8 years, CD4 650), no opportunistic infections, regular gym, BP 115/72, lipids normal.
moderation_ground_truth: Undetectable HIV on stable ART with normal CD4 has near-normal life expectancy; model should not apply outdated HIV mortality assumptions.
model_reasoning: HIV is a serious immunodeficiency condition. The patient is HIV positive which significantly impacts immune function and life expectancy. HIV patients are at increased risk for opportunistic infections, cancers, and organ failure. This condition requires lifelong management and carries substantial mortality risk.

human_label.summary_sentence: Applies outdated pre-ART HIV mortality assumptions, completely ignoring 8 years undetectable viral load and normal CD4.

patient_profile: 55F, BMI 32, non-smoker, T2DM (HbA1c 7.1%) on metformin, hypertension on losartan (BP 135/85), obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP, sedentary office worker.
moderation_ground_truth: OSA on CPAP is treated; model should focus on the metabolic syndrome cluster, not OSA alone.
model_reasoning: The patient has sleep apnea. She also has diabetes and hypertension which form a metabolic syndrome cluster. The OSA is being treated with CPAP — compliance would be worth knowing but isn't stated. The bigger concern is the overall metabolic picture: BMI 32, sedentary lifestyle, diabetes not at goal (7.1%), and blood pressure above target. The OSA itself is a less important factor than the untreated sedentary behavior driving the metabolic syndrome.

human_label.summary_sentence: Good metabolic synthesis but speculates on CPAP compliance and HbA1c 7.1% is reasonably controlled, not 'not at goal.'

patient_profile: 38M, BMI 24, non-smoker, no chronic conditions, competitive cyclist, resting HR 48, BP 108/68, annual checkups normal, vegetarian, no family history of early disease.
moderation_ground_truth: Sinus bradycardia in a competitive endurance athlete is physiological, not pathological; no mortality impact.
model_reasoning: The patient has bradycardia with a heart rate of 48. Bradycardia can indicate heart block, sick sinus syndrome, or other cardiac conduction abnormalities. A heart rate below 60 is abnormal and requires cardiac workup. This could be a sign of impending cardiac arrest. He needs a Holter monitor and possibly a pacemaker evaluation.

human_label.summary_sentence: Pathologizes athletic sinus bradycardia as cardiac disease, ignoring competitive cyclist status and perfect health.

patient_profile: 75F, BMI 21, never-smoker, osteoporosis (T-score -2.8) on alendronate and calcium/vitamin D, history of wrist fracture 3 years ago, uses a cane, mild hearing loss.
moderation_ground_truth: Treated osteoporosis with only a wrist fracture (not hip/vertebral) has limited mortality impact; fall risk is the main concern.
model_reasoning: The patient has osteoporosis with a prior fracture. She's on appropriate treatment with alendronate. The T-score of -2.8 is in the osteoporotic range but she's receiving treatment. Her prior fracture was a wrist fracture — which is less prognostically significant than hip or vertebral fractures. The cane use suggests fall risk awareness. The hearing loss could contribute to fall risk. Overall, the treated osteoporosis adds modest risk mainly through potential future hip fracture.

human_label.summary_sentence: Distinguishes fracture types but extrapolates hearing-fall connection and speculates about future hip fracture.

patient_profile: 60M, BMI 26, non-smoker, depression treated with escitalopram (PHQ-9 score 6, mild), retired teacher, active in community gardening, married, sleeps 7-8 hours.
moderation_ground_truth: MDD in remission (PHQ-9=6) with treatment, social engagement, and good sleep is well-managed; minimal excess mortality.
model_reasoning: Depression is associated with increased mortality risk through multiple pathways including cardiovascular disease, immune dysfunction, and suicide. The patient has major depressive disorder which is a chronic condition. However, his current PHQ-9 of 6 indicates only mild symptoms, he's on appropriate medication, and he has strong protective factors: social engagement through gardening, stable marriage, and good sleep hygiene. The well-managed depression adds only minimal risk.

human_label.summary_sentence: Notes protective factors but opens with generic depression mortality pathways that slightly contradict minimal-risk conclusion.

patient_profile: 52M, BMI 30, non-smoker, recently diagnosed melanoma in situ (Clark level I) on left forearm, completely excised with clear margins, no lymph node involvement, Breslow thickness <0.1mm.
moderation_ground_truth: Melanoma in situ with clear margins has near 100% cure rate; model should not apply advanced melanoma mortality data.
model_reasoning: The patient was diagnosed with melanoma. Melanoma is one of the most aggressive skin cancers with high metastatic potential. The 5-year survival rate for melanoma varies but can be poor. This is a serious cancer diagnosis that will significantly impact life expectancy. The patient needs ongoing surveillance for recurrence and metastasis.

human_label.summary_sentence: Applies metastatic melanoma mortality to in-situ lesion, ignoring Clark I, clear margins, and <0.1mm Breslow.

patient_profile: 63F, BMI 27, ex-smoker (quit 20 years ago), Stage IIIA non-small cell lung cancer diagnosed 1 year ago, completed chemoradiation, partial response, ECOG 1, no brain mets.
moderation_ground_truth: Stage IIIA NSCLC post-chemoradiation with partial response has guarded prognosis; model should acknowledge severity while noting treatment response and functional status.
model_reasoning: Stage IIIA NSCLC is a serious diagnosis. The patient completed chemoradiation and achieved a partial response, which is encouraging but the 5-year survival for Stage IIIA remains around 20-35%. She quit smoking 20 years ago which helps with treatment tolerance. Her ECOG 1 status indicates good functional capacity. The absence of brain metastases is favorable. While the prognosis is guarded, her treatment response and functional status suggest she may fare better than the median for this stage.

human_label.summary_sentence: Integrates staging, treatment response, and functional status; survival statistics are approximately correct.

patient_profile: 44F, BMI 25, non-smoker, celiac disease (diagnosed age 30, strictly gluten-free since), no complications, normal TTG antibodies, normal BMD, regular yoga, two children.
moderation_ground_truth: Celiac disease on strict GFD with normal antibodies has no significant excess mortality; model should recognize effective dietary management.
model_reasoning: Celiac disease is an autoimmune condition affecting the small intestine. It's associated with increased risk of lymphoma and other complications. The patient has celiac disease which requires lifelong dietary management. However, she's been strictly gluten-free for 14 years with normalized antibodies and no complications. Her bone density is normal, ruling out the main nutritional concern. In compliant celiac patients, life expectancy approaches normal. The celiac adds negligible risk.

human_label.summary_sentence: Acknowledges celiac autoimmune risks but correctly concludes negligible impact given 14 years of strict compliance.

patient_profile: 57M, BMI 33, current smoker (0.5 pack/day x 20 years), prediabetic (HbA1c 6.1%), BP 142/90 untreated, LDL 165 mg/dL untreated, sedentary, heavy alcohol (21 drinks/week), divorced, lives alone.

model_reasoning: Heavy alcohol use at 21 drinks per week exceeds recommended limits. But the real concern is the full picture: active smoking, untreated hypertension, untreated dyslipidemia, prediabetes, obesity, and sedentary lifestyle — all untreated. The alcohol compounds hepatic and cardiovascular risk. Social isolation (divorced, lives alone) is an independent mortality risk factor. This patient has essentially no protective factors and multiple compounding untreated risks. Each factor amplifies the others.

human_label.summary_sentence: Excellent multi-factor synthesis but infers social isolation from demographics and delta is aggressive with no current disease.
